perturbation.original_text: The term “Confidential Information” shall include all Hydranautics’ trade secrets and information that conc erns the confidential business or affairs of Hydranautics and Nitto including without limitation, financial information and reports, business plans, design and construction plans, manufacturing processes, customer lists, employee data, forecasts, strategies, and all other proprietary business information.
perturbation.changed_text: The term “Confidential Information” shall include information Hydranautics deems confidential, regardless of whether it qualifies as a trade secret or concerns the business affairs of Hydranautics and Nitto including without limitation, financial information and reports, business plans, design and construction plans, manufacturing processes, customer lists, employee data, forecasts, strategies, and all other business information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement that confidential information must concern the 'business or affairs' of the company and stating that it includes information Hydranautics 'deems confidential,' the definition is broadened beyond the scope of legally protected trade secrets or confidential business information.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. The modified text allows Hydranautics to designate any information as 'Confidential Information,' regardless of its economic value or whether reasonable secrecy efforts are in place, conflicting with the statutory definition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract broadens the definition of "Confidential Information" beyond legally protected trade secrets by stating that it includes information Hydranautics 'deems confidential,' regardless of its economic value or reasonable secrecy efforts. This contradicts CUTSA, which, as explained in the Law Explanation, defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' in relation to 'trade secrets,' reinforcing the importance of adhering to the legally recognized definition of a trade secret. The changed text deviates from this legal standard, making the contract unenforceable to the extent that it attempts to protect information that doesn't qualify as a trade secret under CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: Visitor agrees to hold the Confidential Information in the strictest confidence, and will exercise at least the same care with respect thereto as it exercises with respect to its’ own confidential or proprietary information and will not without Hydranautics’ consent use, divulge, co py, release, sell, loan or otherwise reveal such Confidential Information to others.
perturbation.changed_text: Visitor agrees to hold the Confidential Information in reasonable confidence, but is not obligated to a standard of care higher than what is customary in the industry, and will not without Hydranautics’ direct written consent, deliberately use, divulge, copy, release, sell, loan or otherwise reveal such Confidential Information to outside parties.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'strictest confidence' to 'reasonable confidence' and the introduction of 'customary in the industry' weakens the standard of care and introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Civil Code regarding fiduciary duty and confidentiality
perturbation.law_explanation: California law implies a duty to abstain from injuring others and violating private rights (like trade secrets). Reducing the standard of care to 'reasonable confidence' and only prohibiting 'deliberate' disclosure may fall below the required standard of care for protecting confidential information, particularly if the information is legally considered a trade secret or subject to a specific confidentiality agreement requiring a higher degree of protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1708. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1708. Every person is bound, without contract, to abstain from injuring the person or property of another, or infringing upon any of his or her rights. (Amended by Stats. 2002, Ch. 664, Sec. 38.5. Effective January 1, 2003.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the standard of care for protecting confidential information, potentially contradicting California Civil Code Section 1708. The original text requires 'strictest confidence,' while the modified text requires 'reasonable confidence' and is 'not obligated to a standard of care higher than what is customary in the industry.' This potentially allows for a lower level of protection than required by law. Snippet 1 states that 'Every person is bound, without contract, to abstain from injuring the person or property of another, or infringing upon any of his or her rights.' Reducing the standard of care could lead to a breach of the duty to protect confidential information, infringing on the owner's rights.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire Agre ement between the parties hereto with respect to the subject matter.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties hereto with respect to the subject matter, except for any implied duties of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding implied duties of good faith and fair dealing, the contract attempts to override established legal principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Commercial Code regarding implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: California law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Attempting to exclude this implied duty is generally unenforceable, especially if it would allow one party to act in bad faith or deprive the other party of the benefits of the agreement. Therefore, it creates a contradiction with the legal standard.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person
perturbation.justification: The modified contract explicitly excludes implied duties of good faith and fair dealing, which the Law Explanation states is generally unenforceable in California law. The original text implies that the entire agreement is within the contract's written terms. By excluding 'any implied duties of good faith and fair dealing,' the changed text attempts to waive a legal protection, creating a contradiction. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 provides no data, Scraped Law Snippet 1, COM 1201, defines 'Agreement' as the bargain of the parties, inferred from their language or other circumstances. Excluding good faith and fair dealing attempts to narrow this definition in a way that contradicts established legal precedent, per the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, excluding its conflicts of law principles and the Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.explanation: By excluding the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), the contract attempts to bypass established legal protections for trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) provides a legal framework for protecting trade secrets. Excluding CUTSA from the governing law creates a contradiction because it removes the statutory protections afforded to trade secrets, leaving the definition and enforcement of confidentiality ambiguous and potentially unenforceable under state law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text CIV Civil Code - CIV
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text explicitly excludes the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), a body of law that provides a legal framework for protecting trade secrets. This exclusion directly contradicts the implicit requirement that trade secrets be legally protected, creating a significant contradiction. The Law Explanation states that excluding CUTSA 'removes the statutory protections afforded to trade secrets, leaving the definition and enforcement of confidentiality ambiguous and potentially unenforceable under state law.' The modified text removes a key protection, undermining the legally established standard for trade secret protection in California. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'CIV Civil Code - CIV', which suggests the existence of a civil code that could include provisions related to trade secrets, further implying the importance of adhering to established legal frameworks like UTSA.
